provenance.source_corpus: Evaluating reintroduction methods for the Gulf of Saint Lawrence aster ( Symphyotrichum laurentianum ) on Prince Edward Island

nodes.name: Seeding and the transplantation of greenhouse-grown seedlings

source_spans.text: Promising results related to seeding and the transplantation of greenhouse-grown seedlings at four in situ sites demonstrated that SLA plantlets have the potential to serve as seed stock to re-establish populations.

Promising results related to seeding and the transplantation of greenhouse-grown seedlings at four in situ sites demonstrated that SLA plantlets have the potential to serve as seed stock to re-establish populations.
The survivorship results indicate that transplants did not benefit from the removal of vegetation to lower competition levels, and that simulating potholes had a detrimental effect on populations.
The survivorship results indicate that transplants did not benefit from the removal of vegetation to lower competition levels, and that simulating potholes had a detrimental effect on populations.
higher germination was noted to take place in the areas where vegetation underwent some form of removal
higher germination was noted to take place in the areas where vegetation underwent some form of removal
Competition for light can have negative influences on this plant's development

germination is reduced once salt concentrations reach 10 g/L

delays in germination resulting from exposure to low soil temperatures could set up strong size asymmetries between SLA seedlings and surrounding vegetation, resulting in a suppression of SLA population growth due to shading

delays in germination resulting from exposure to low soil temperatures could set up strong size asymmetries between SLA seedlings and surrounding vegetation, resulting in a suppression of SLA population growth due to shading
ground temperature may not be optimal for SLA establishment throughout the germination season at the sites tested within PEINP
